sentences.text: Yeah.
I went in to the Bergmeister.
You know what a Bergmeister is?
Yeah, like the mayor of the village.
And I told him I was escaping.
The Russians were coming east.
I was escaping.
I'm a Volksdeutsche.
Those words I'll never forget.
And I told him my name, and he says, join the army.
I said, I can't.
Look at my foot.
Look what the Russians did.
He took me in, and I went to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked there, how long, I don't know I was--
Yeah.
I don't know how long I was there, but I do remember the day was May the 15th.
That I remember.
And the Russians came in.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And being that I speak Polish, I was able to talk some Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do speak it better now, but at that time, I didn't.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they went wild, and so did I.
I didn't kill anybody.
I didn't have any strength.
But we were screaming, hollering, you name it.
What the Russians did, forget about it.
I ain't going to tell you.
Because even for me to see it, it was gruesome at that time.
But I enjoyed it, because it-- it was coming to them.
They did it to me.
Now somebody was doing it to them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know better now, but at that time that was enjoyable to see them-- somebody cry, not only me, not only my family.
To do to somebody else what somebody had done to you.
Yeah.
They didn't do that, but close, you know?
They-- they retaliated for a few days.
They let the steam go, and then it was fine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the few of us who were in the village decided to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do something, see something-- see where you are, what you come, what you do.
And we didn't know where we were-- what part of the world.
We knew it was Teplice-Sanov, who knew?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the German Czech border.
We didn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we started to-- to walk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and from village to village.
We were lucky we found a lot of bicycles.
See, the German soldiers didn't want to be caught, and if you were caught with a German bicycle, they knew that you were a soldier.
So they would drop the bicycles.
We would pick them up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember I had one, and we just peddled.
Plain clothes.
To be honest with you, we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stole a lot of clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember one thing that I'll never forget, and until today I feel bad about it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I stole a suitcase full of stamps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gave it away later, but I stole it.
Why, I don't know.
What were stamps?
I don't know.
I never saw a stamp in my life.
I never mailed a letter in my life.
I never wrote a letter in my life.
There was a suitcase full of-- I picked it up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
On the back of the bicycles, there were, like, luggage things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Funny, you know, it reminded me-- those stamps [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was passage to the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came illegally, but it was passage.
And we travelled--
When I ended up in Antwerp, Belgium, I didn't speak English.
That was one language that I didn't speak.
But somehow or other, I got together with two sailors, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] God bless them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They wanted to help me, but they wanted something.
So I said here, that's all I have.
Take it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took it.
Took me on a ship, put me in a hole.
That, I remember.
For 17 days I ate orange marmalade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Hoboken, New Jersey.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To get me off the ship, they dressed me in a sailor's uniform, merchant marine-- you know, regular clothes-- and told me to act like a drunk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They held me up and took me out of the port.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gave me $0.50.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's all I remember.
I never saw them again.
They left me on the street-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] funny story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had $0.50, and I wanted to eat something.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, remember, I lived in Europe for a few months, all over, so I was a little bit, you know, knowing what life is all about.
Hopefully, I went into this restaurant and I saw-- I couldn't read English, remember-- I saw a sign that says $0.35, that I knew.
I said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she brought me ham and eggs.
I'll never forget it.
And I ate it.
I gave her the $0.50, and she gave me a nickel and a dime tip-- I mean, back.
I wanted to give the lady a tip, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I put down the dime.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I didn't know which one is bigger, the dime or the nickel, which is more money.
And I wanted to give her a bigger tip, so I grabbed the dime back and gave her the nickel.
[LAUGHS] Later I found out that's what it is.
Stories-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's one of the stories.
Anyway, we travelled all over Europe.
A bunch of guys.
Yeah, a bunch of guys, a bunch of--
No, no, no, no, no.
I'm not talking about here.
I'm talking in Europe.
I'm talking when I was in Europe.
Yeah, we-- we went to Paris, Italy, Milano, Modena, Bologna, all over.
We went to Bari, Napoli, Austria.
We travelled the free world.
No mother, no father, nobody to tell you what to do.
No government, no German to tell you, I'm going to kill you if you don't do this.
Nothing.
You were free.
So, we didn't hurt anybody.
We just travelled.
The borders were open.
At the end, the borders were closed, but you can get by.
I remember in Paris there was a place called [FRENCH].
Still is there.
I just saw it when I was there last time.
Right now it's an [INAUDIBLE] factory.
When I was there, it was a camp for people like me.
[FRENCH] And they gave out, to people like me, what was called a [FRENCH].

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This card was like a-- like a passport, that you can go anyplace in any border-- they'll let you go through until you end up in your country that you came from, which I never did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we were in Holland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember joining the Jewish Brigade.
You know what that is?
No.
When the Polish army left Poland, under the [INAUDIBLE] army that ended up in England, they ended up in Egypt before they went to England.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And about 15,000 of them were Jews in the Polish army.
They decided to go to Palestine at that time.
The other army, the Poles, went to England.
The Jews went to Palestine.
After a while, the British formed-- now I know what they did, I didn't know then-- they formed what is called-- it was called the Jewish brigade.
They fought in Germany under the British flag, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every Jewish soldier had a Star of David over here, with a signet saying Jewish Brigade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I joined them in Belgium.
They wouldn't take me, but I got a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] army pay book.
I was too young.
What they were doing was sending people back to Palestine at that time.
And I got a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] army pay book.
This pay book was so that I can go to Israel, and the British would let me in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I was a returning soldier.
But I was 16, 17.
Nah.
We didn't think of girls.
Not yet.
I was too young.
The Jewish brigade was in Belgium, which I was with the one in Holland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day I-- I decided to come to the United States.
This is what I told you, about those two-- two people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, when I came to United States, I always knew I had an aunt over here, and I had an uncle.
And the reason I knew is because we got matzoh for Passover when we were in Europe.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
My mother's sister and my mother's brother.
One turns out to be lived in LA, and the other one lived in-- in New York.
My auntie's name was [?
Gussie, ?] and my uncle's name was [?
Salman. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a couple of children here.
One of them, as I know of now, lives in Phoenix, and my Auntie [?
Gussie ?] never had any children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when I was taken off that boat in Hoboken, New Jersey, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- I didn't know what to do.
I didn't know what to do.
I didn't speak English.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I saw a grocery store-- big window, like a supermarket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember even today, and this was Saturday night.
And I was standing in the front of the door trying to contact this man, to talk to him, in any language that I knew, that he knew.
And I thought that he was Italian, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I spoke Italian.
So, I have to trust somebody.
I didn't know even what illegal means.
I just had to go to some Jewish place-- somebody that is Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This man was nice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He took me home, gave me a shower, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clothes.
The next day he took me to New York-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this was Sunday-- to a place called HIAS.
You know what the HIAS is, right?
What's the-- Hyatt?
HIAS.
HIAS-- I don't know if it still exists.
It was a Jewish organization that helped people like me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had an office in New York, and they did some good things, I guess.
They helped me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took me there on Sunday.
It was closed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but some of the offices were open.
And they took me in and they kept me there overnight.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Monday morning, when they all came to work, there was a big social hall-- like a room, like-- there was about 30, 40 kids in there.
I was one of them.
I was interviewed in Yiddish, naturally.
I didn't speak English.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they asked me when I come from, and I told them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now I know, they found out-- I guess, in New York, they have a society of each little village that came from Poland, or from any place in the world.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lady comes over to me, she says, I know your aunt.
[SOBBING] I said, no, you can't know that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was right.
My-- about two hours later, a lady comes into the room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, you know, she walks over, looks all the way around, and she walks over to me.
She says, you're the one, in Yiddish.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That was my first [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] day of life in the United States.
And the rest--
--is history.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nice lady, tallish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw her once after that.
I don't know.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was heavy set.
My mother was not.
That I do remember.
They lived in Brooklyn, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the rest is history.
Yeah.
Well, I mean, I stayed with her.
After that, I saw her once.
I stayed with her for a few months.
And like all the Jewish mothers, they want to smother you with love and kisses, and I was not into being smothered.
I was-- I was free for so long, so many months, and she wanted to-- I should be with her, stay with her.
I couldn't.
I went to-- I hitchhiked to a place called Lake George, New York.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll never forget it.
They told me they needed a person that can cook, and there I was.
I didn't have any experience, but I can cook.
If you want to do something, you do it.
I can cook.
I didn't learn-- I didn't know then, but I learned after that.
And I got a job in a spaghetti place, making spaghetti.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's where I was introduced to a form of antisemitism in the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a hotel [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the city with a big sign by the door-- no Jews and dogs allowed.
I came here in '46.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This free country, that I stayed 17 days in a hole, shows me no Jews and dogs allowed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But being a nice boy like my mother told me to be, I-- I say, then that, too, will pass.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From Lake George, New York, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to a place called Saratoga Springs.
I didn't know where I was going, but they told there was a job there.
People.
Saratoga Springs was-- is a racetrack kind of place-- a country with racetracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hitchhiked there, got a job in the latest place as a bellhop, carrying the suitcases into the room.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got a place to stay.
I made $12 a week.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nice-- I didn't have to spend it.
I had food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how you call it.
It's called a mineral water.
It stinks to high heaven.
And a lot of people came there for the water.
Yeah.
And I figured out how to get them the water.
I got together bottles, went on a bike to the well where this stinking water was, brought it to their doors, and in the morning when they woke up there was a bottle of water.
They didn't have to go and get it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That reminds me of another-- I've got another man there, that reminds me.
A nice man.
I don't know-- I forgot his name.
He came into Saratoga just to gamble, and he told me, every morning take the clothes, go to the cleaner-- I already understood a little bit of English-- take to the cleaner, and bring them back.
Take a cab.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Take a cab?
I took a bicycle, went to the town.
He thought I took a cab.
I brought him these clothes.
I had to be there before 11:00 because he had to go to the racetrack at 12:00.
And he, with his closing eyes, would open up his hand and stick in his money-- and give me money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must have gotten a thousand dollars from this man.
It was four weeks there.
Beautiful [? man. ?]
I also made money selling cookies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At night they would play cards-- nothing else to do.
So I would steal cookies from the kitchen during the day, and tea, and coffee.
And while they were playing cards I served them and they would give me some money.
It was nice.
It was a good life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then when I came back to New York, my aunt wanted me to give her the money, go to school, do this, do that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said no.
Maybe I should have.
I don't know.
I came to LA, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I met my-- my uncle was dead already, I met my cousins.
I stayed with them a little while.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went on my own.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided I wanted to go to school.
I went to Venice High, night school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they told me that I don't have to go to school.
I can get a diploma right away.
I said, why?
They said, because every language you know, [? you get ?] credit for it.
I told the counselor there, I don't need credits, I need--
I need to know how to speak, read, and write English, nothing else.
I need some history, but I don't need biology all kinds of stuff.
Just teach me how to speak, read, and write in English.
He says, you're past that stage.
I said, OK.
So I didn't go back to school.
I was there for one semester.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in order to learn how to read and write, I would take a newspaper.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of the time didn't know what I was saying or doing, and somebody told me to pronounce the words with my mouth right.
So I would sit in the bathroom, look in the mirror so I can see what I'm saying, and for hours I would sit there and read and read.
And I didn't know what I was saying, but it was fun.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Then I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got a job at Thrifty's [?
Rockstall ?], [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and-- Were you still on your own?
Yeah, I was still on my own.
Still on my own.
I lived in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived in somebody's home, in a room.
They took me in like I was their kid.
